scenario_description: A sequence of images depicts an orchid's lifecycle, starting with a bare stem, progressing to full bloom, and ending with a pruned stem showing new side growth. The user must deduce the chronological order and explain the causal link between pruning and the change in growth direction.

turns.text: Here is a picture of my orchid right now. It looks pretty tall!
turns.image_description: A close-up shot of a potted Phalaenopsis orchid. A long, sturdy green spike rises vertically from the center of the pot, reaching about 12 inches high. At the top third of this spike, five large, vibrant white flowers with yellow centers are fully open. The leaves at the base are healthy and dark green. The background is a blurred living room setting.

turns.text: That looks beautiful! Those blooms are really striking on that tall spike. Did it just start blooming recently?
Actually, here's what it looked like before all that happened. Just a plain stick.
turns.image_description: The same potted orchid in the same lighting and location. A thin, green stem protrudes from the leaf axil near the base of the plant. The stem is short (about 4 inches) and completely bare, with no buds or flowers visible. The tip of the stem is rounded and closed, indicating it is still growing upward.

turns.text: Wow, quite a transformation from a bare stick to those big flowers! So you let it grow out fully first?
Well, I did something to it after the flowers were done. Look at this one.
turns.image_description: The same orchid pot. The main vertical spike has been cut horizontally, leaving a stub only about 3 inches tall. Two small, fuzzy green nubs (side shoots or keikis) are emerging from nodes just below the cut line on the stub. There are no flowers on the main spike anymore.

turns.text: Interesting! You definitely changed its shape there. It looks like cutting it back triggered those little side shoots to pop out instead of continuing up.

Based on these three stages, why did the plant start growing sideways instead of upwards in the final image? A) The plant ran out of nutrients so it stopped growing tall. B) The removal of the main flower spike removed apical dominance, allowing lateral buds to activate. C) The cold weather forced the plant to hibernate and grow horizontally. D) The pot was too small, forcing the roots to push the stem sideways.

ground_truth.reasoning_chain: 1. Visual ordering: Image 3 shows the initial state (bare stem). Image 1 shows the intermediate state (full bloom on tall spike). Image 5 shows the final state (pruned spike with side shoots). 2. Causal inference: Comparing Image 1 (intact tall spike) and Image 5 (cut spike), the physical act of cutting the top of the stem occurred between these states. 3. Biological principle: In plants, the apical bud produces auxin which suppresses lateral bud growth (apical dominance). Removing the apex (as seen by the cut in Image 5) releases this suppression, causing lateral buds to grow into side shoots, explaining the change in growth direction from vertical to horizontal/side.
ground_truth.key_difficulty: The model must correctly infer the non-chronological order of the images (Bare -> Bloomed -> Pruned) to establish that the cutting event caused the side growth. If the model assumes the images are chronological as presented (Bloomed -> Bare -> Pruned), the causal logic fails. Additionally, the answer requires connecting the visual evidence of the 'cut' to the biological concept of apical dominance, rather than guessing based on environmental factors not shown in the images.

_meta.scenario: A potted orchid is presented in a timeline where it initially has no flowers, then suddenly displays a tall spike with multiple blooms, followed by a frame where the spike is cut short and new side shoots appear. The user must explain why the plant's growth direction changed after the cutting event.
